topic: Extremist Phase, Swadeshi Movement & Surat Split (1905–1916)

questions.text: Bal Gangadhar Tilak is associated with the title 'Lokmanya' and the slogan 'Swaraj is my birthright.' Which of the following newspapers did he edit?
options: Kesari and New India
Kesari and Mahratta
Bengalee and Mahratta
Young India and Kesari
questions.explanation: Bal Gangadhar Tilak edited two newspapers: Kesari (meaning 'Lion', published in Marathi — reaching the common Marathi-speaking people) and Mahratta (published in English — for the educated class). Both were published from Pune. Tilak used these papers to spread nationalist ideas, criticise British policy, and inspire mass agitation. His articles in Kesari earned him sedition charges twice — in 1897 and 1908 — and his 1908 prosecution led to six years' imprisonment in Mandalay, Burma. 'New India' was Annie Besant's newspaper. 'Young India' was associated with Gandhi. 'Bengalee' was Surendranath Banerjee's paper.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Tilak's two newspapers (Kesari in Marathi and Mahratta in English) is a standard fact tested in newspaper-to-leader matching questions

questions.text: The Swadeshi Movement (1905) was triggered by which event, and what was its primary economic demand?
options: Partition of Punjab (1905); demand for free trade between Indian provinces
Partition of Bengal (1905); boycott of British goods and promotion of Indian-made goods
Jallianwala Bagh massacre (1919); demand for Indian-owned banks and factories
Simon Commission (1928); boycott of government schools and courts
questions.explanation: The Swadeshi Movement was triggered by the Partition of Bengal on 16 October 1905 — Curzon's division of Bengal into East Bengal and Assam (Muslim majority) and West Bengal (Hindu majority), widely seen as 'divide and rule.' The boycott resolution was formally passed on 7 August 1905 (before the actual partition). The movement's two economic demands: (1) Boycott British goods — particularly Manchester cloth — including bonfires of foreign cloth and picketing shops; (2) Swadeshi (of one's own country) — promote and use Indian-made goods, supporting Indian textile mills, banks, and enterprises. This was the first time economic nationalism was translated into direct popular action rather than academic argument.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Swadeshi Movement trigger (Partition of Bengal 1905) and primary demand (boycott + Indian goods) is a standard cause-and-demand question

questions.text: The Surat Split of 1907 divided the Indian National Congress into Moderates and Extremists. What was the immediate trigger for the split at the Surat session?
options: Extremists demanded that Congress support the Russian Revolution; Moderates refused
A dispute over the election of the Congress President — Extremists wanted Tilak; Moderates elected Rash Behari Ghosh — led to physical disruption of the session
Moderates wanted to accept the Morley-Minto Reforms; Extremists rejected them
The British government dissolved the Congress; Moderates agreed while Extremists protested
questions.explanation: The immediate trigger of the Surat Split was a dispute over who would be Congress President at the 1907 Surat session. Extremists (led by Tilak) wanted Tilak to be President; Moderates (led by Pherozeshah Mehta, Gokhale) maneuvered to elect their own candidate, Rash Behari Ghosh. When proceedings began and Rash Behari Ghosh was declared President, Tilak's supporters (particularly from Maharashtra) disrupted the session — shoes were thrown, microphones pulled away, physical altercations broke out. The session dissolved in chaos. The deeper cause was the fundamental disagreement on methods (constitutional vs. mass agitation) and goals (gradual reform vs. immediate Swaraj) that had been building since 1905.
questions.source: SSC CGL — the Surat Split (1907) and its trigger (Congress President dispute; Rash Behari Ghosh vs. Tilak) is a standard cause question

questions.text: Lala Lajpat Rai died in November 1928 following which incident?
options: He was shot by a British officer during the Non-Cooperation Movement
He was injured in a lathi charge by police during a protest against the all-British Simon Commission in Lahore
He died in a prison hunger strike after being arrested for leading the Swadeshi Movement
He was killed in the Jallianwala Bagh massacre in 1919
questions.explanation: Lala Lajpat Rai (Punjab Kesari / Sher-e-Punjab) died on 17 November 1928, following injuries sustained during a lathi (baton) charge by police at Lahore. He had led a peaceful protest march against the Simon Commission — an all-British committee appointed to review India's constitutional situation, which had no Indian members ('Simon Go Back' was the slogan). Police Superintendent James Scott ordered the charge; Lajpat Rai was badly beaten. He died of his injuries 18 days later. His last public words were 'Every blow that falls on my body will be a nail in the coffin of British imperialism.' His death directly inspired Bhagat Singh, Rajguru, and Sukhdev to plan revenge — they shot dead police officer J.P. Saunders (whom they mistook for Scott) in December 1928.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Lala Lajpat Rai's death (Simon Commission lathi charge, 1928) is a standard fact tested repeatedly; also linked to Bhagat Singh questions

questions.text: Tilak used two annual festivals — Ganpati and Shivaji Birthday — as tools of political mobilization. What was the political insight behind using religious/cultural events for nationalist purposes?
options: Tilak wanted to Hinduise the Congress party and drive out its Muslim members

Religious and cultural events could gather large crowds that the British couldn't ban without appearing anti-Hindu; they bypassed literacy and reached people who didn't read newspapers, building a mass political base impossible through English-medium journalism
Tilak needed religious legitimacy for his political ambitions — the festivals were purely personal
The British had banned all political meetings, so religious gatherings were the only legal way to assemble people
questions.explanation: Tilak's insight was profound: the Moderates' political methods worked only for the educated English-reading minority — maybe 2-3% of India's population. To build a mass movement, you needed to reach the other 97%. Religious and cultural events offered: (1) Legality — the British could not ban Hindu religious celebrations without provoking massive backlash and appearing to attack Hinduism; (2) Scale — they gathered thousands who would never attend a political meeting; (3) Accessibility — speeches could be in Marathi, accessible to farmers and workers; (4) Emotional resonance — religious devotion and historical heroes (Shivaji) created genuine passion. The Ganpati festival (1893) and Shivaji celebrations (1896) were experiments in what would later be called 'mass politics.' Gandhi learned from Tilak's example when he later mobilized India's peasantry.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the political rationale behind Tilak's use of festivals (mass mobilization beyond the educated elite) is an analytical question in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: Aurobindo Ghosh was a key figure in both the Swadeshi movement and the Extremist faction. What happened to him after the Alipore Bomb Case (1908)?
options: He was executed by the British for his role in planning bomb attacks
He was acquitted in the Alipore case, then in 1910 suddenly left politics, moved to Pondicherry (French India), and devoted himself entirely to spiritual philosophy — founding his ashram there
He went to England, where he joined the India Home Rule lobby in Parliament
He was deported to the Andaman Islands and spent 10 years in the Cellular Jail
questions.explanation: The Alipore Bomb Case (1908-1909) arose from the Muzaffarpur bombing by Khudiram Bose and Prafulla Chaki (who threw a bomb at a carriage believing it carried the local judge — killed two British women by mistake). The investigations led to a wider network — the Maniktala Garden of Barindra Kumar Ghosh (Aurobindo's brother) — where revolutionary activity was centred. Aurobindo was arrested. The defence lawyer was C.R. Das. Aurobindo was acquitted (1909). But in 1910, after a spiritual experience, he suddenly moved to Pondicherry (then French territory, outside British jurisdiction) and renounced politics entirely — devoting himself to yoga and philosophy. He founded his Ashram there and wrote major philosophical works (The Life Divine, Savitri). His political withdrawal was as dramatic as his political entry.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because Aurobindo's transition from revolutionary politics to spiritual philosophy (Pondicherry Ashram) is a specific outcome question that tests knowledge beyond the Alipore case verdict

questions.text: The Lucknow Pact (1916) is seen as a moment of Congress-League unity, but also as a Congress concession that had long-term negative consequences. What was the concession, and why is it seen as problematic?
options: Congress agreed to give the Muslim League veto power over all legislation — which paralysed government later

Congress accepted separate electorates for Muslims — legitimising the idea that Muslims must vote separately for Muslim candidates, which institutionalised communal identity in politics and provided a precedent for Partition arguments
Congress agreed to divide India into Hindu and Muslim majority zones, with different laws in each
The pact required that any future independence constitution give Muslims 50% of all seats regardless of population
questions.explanation: The Lucknow Pact's key Congress concession was accepting separate electorates for Muslims — Muslims would vote in separate constituencies to elect Muslim-only candidates, with guaranteed minimum seats in each legislature. The Morley-Minto Reforms (1909) had introduced this, and Congress had opposed it then. By accepting it in the Lucknow Pact, Congress legitimised separate electorates as a political principle. Long-term consequences: (1) It entrenched communal identity — Muslims were constitutionally defined as a separate political community, not just citizens; (2) It gave the Muslim League leverage — if they had separate seats, they had separate interests; (3) It provided a logical precedent: if Muslims had separate electorates in united India, why not a separate state? Critics argued the Lucknow Pact's Congress concession helped lay the groundwork for the two-nation theory that produced Pakistan.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the Lucknow Pact's long-term consequences (separate electorates as precedent for Partition) are a specific analytical question in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: Tilak wrote 'Gita Rahasya' while imprisoned in Mandalay (1908-1914). What was its central argument?
options: The Gita teaches complete withdrawal from the world into meditation — Indians should become spiritual, not political
The Gita's central teaching is Nishkama Karma — selfless action in the world; it was a call to engage, not renounce; implicitly justifying political activism as a sacred duty
The Gita proves that India was a great nation before the British came, and its revival requires returning to Vedic civilization
The Gita teaches that violence in the service of justice is permissible — justifying armed revolution against British rule
questions.explanation: Gita Rahasya ('The Secret of the Gita', written 1910-11, published 1915) was Tilak's philosophical magnum opus. Its central argument: the Bhagavad Gita's true message is Karma Yoga — selfless, disciplined action in the world — not renunciation or passive spirituality. The dominant interpretation at the time (influenced by Shankaracharya's Advaita tradition) emphasised jnana (knowledge) and vairagya (detachment) — which Tilak believed had made Hindus passive and accepting of oppression. Tilak argued Krishna's teaching to Arjuna was 'act fearlessly for what is right' — actively and selflessly engage with the world. Written in a Burmese prison, the subtext was clear: political action for Swaraj is a sacred duty, not a worldly distraction. The book became one of the most important works of Indian philosophy in the 20th century.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Gita Rahasya appears in basic facts (Tilak wrote it in Mandalay) but its content and significance are rarely explained; appears in philosophy-history crossover questions

questions.text: Rabindranath Tagore's response to the Partition of Bengal included organizing 'Rakhi Bandhan.' What was this and what was its significance?
options: Tagore wrote a formal legal petition against the Partition and gathered a million signatures — the 'Rakhi Bandhan' of solidarity around the document
Tagore organized Hindus and Muslims to tie rakhis (sacred threads) on each other as a symbol of unity — directly countering Curzon's goal of dividing Hindus and Muslims
Tagore led a hunger strike (rakhi = fast in Bengali) against the Partition until the British agreed to reconsider
Tagore founded the Rakhi Samiti, a women's organization that organized boycotts of British cloth
questions.explanation: Rabindranath Tagore organized a 'Rakhi Bandhan' (tying of rakhis — sacred threads traditionally tied by sisters on brothers' wrists) on 16 October 1905 — the day the Partition took effect. The idea was for Hindus and Muslims to tie rakhis on each other as a symbol of brotherhood and unity, directly countering Curzon's strategy of dividing the two communities. Tagore also composed songs (including 'Amar Sonar Bangla' — 'My Golden Bengal' — which later became Bangladesh's national anthem) for the movement. His contribution was primarily cultural and symbolic rather than organizational. He also inaugurated the boycott day with a mass procession in Calcutta. Later, however, Tagore grew uncomfortable with the movement's increasingly aggressive religious nationalism and its impact on Hindu-Muslim relations — a critique he made in his 1916 novel 'Ghare Baire' (The Home and the World).
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because the Rakhi Bandhan detail is often taught as a one-line fact; the Tagore connection ('Amar Sonar Bangla' → Bangladesh anthem) and his later critique of the movement are specific knowledge points

questions.text: Bipin Chandra Pal is called the 'Father of Revolutionary Thought in India.' What earned him this title, and what happened to his political role after 1907?
options: He founded the Anushilan Samiti and organised the Muzaffarpur bombing — making him the father of armed revolution

He articulated a vision of nationalist struggle rooted in spiritual awakening and mass consciousness — a more radical departure from Moderate constitutionalism; after 1907, he gradually withdrew from active politics and effectively retired by 1911
He was the first Indian to use the term 'independence' instead of 'self-government' — coining the revolutionary idea of complete separation from Britain
He fought in the 1857 Revolt as a child and became the living link between 1857 and modern nationalism
questions.explanation: Bipin Chandra Pal (1858-1932) earned the title 'Father of Revolutionary Thought in India' not for organizing violence but for articulating a vision that went beyond both Moderate constitutionalism and even Tilak's pragmatic mass politics — connecting Indian nationalism to spiritual awakening, cultural regeneration, and the full assertion of Indian selfhood. His oratory was electrifying. He saw India's liberation as requiring a complete inner transformation, not just political maneuvers. After the Surat Split (1907) and Aurobindo's withdrawal (1910), the Bengali Extremist movement lost momentum. Pal was imprisoned briefly for contempt of court (1907). He gradually moved toward more moderate positions and had largely withdrawn from active politics by 1911 — becoming a political commentator and journalist rather than a movement leader. Of the three 'Lal-Bal-Pal', he had the shortest political career at the top.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Bipin Chandra Pal is taught as the 'third member of Lal-Bal-Pal' but his specific title ('Father of Revolutionary Thought') and his post-1907 decline are rarely explained

questions.text:
Match the leader with their associated title or contribution:
1. Bal Gangadhar Tilak    A. Father of Revolutionary Thought
2. Lala Lajpat Rai        B. Lokmanya; Gita Rahasya
3. Bipin Chandra Pal      C. Ambassador of Hindu-Muslim Unity
4. M.A. Jinnah            D. Punjab Kesari
questions.explanation: Correct matches: (1) Bal Gangadhar Tilak → B. 'Lokmanya' (title meaning 'Accepted by the People') and wrote Gita Rahasya in Mandalay prison; (2) Lala Lajpat Rai → D. 'Punjab Kesari' (Lion of Punjab) and 'Sher-e-Punjab'; (3) Bipin Chandra Pal → A. 'Father of Revolutionary Thought in India' (for his radical nationalist vision rooted in spiritual awakening); (4) M.A. Jinnah → C. 'Ambassador of Hindu-Muslim Unity' (title given by Sarojini Naidu for his role in the Lucknow Pact of 1916). This Lal-Bal-Pal + Jinnah matching is one of the most frequently tested formats combining the Extremist phase with the Lucknow Pact.
questions.source: Title/contribution matching for Lal-Bal-Pal and Jinnah is the most frequently tested question format for this topic in SSC CGL

questions.text: Which of the following correctly matches Tilak's Home Rule League and Annie Besant's Home Rule League with their founding details?
options: Tilak: September 1916, Adyar; Besant: April 1916, Poona
Tilak: April 1916, Poona; Besant: September 1916, Adyar
Tilak: 1915, Bombay; Besant: 1917, Calcutta
Both were founded simultaneously in Bombay in 1916 as a joint organisation
questions.explanation: There were two separate Home Rule Leagues in 1916: (1) Tilak's Home Rule League — founded in April 1916, headquartered at Poona (Pune), active in Maharashtra, Karnataka, Central Provinces, and Berar; (2) Annie Besant's Home Rule League — founded in September 1916, headquartered at Adyar, Madras (HQ of the Theosophical Society), active in the rest of India. Both demanded self-government (Home Rule) within the British Empire. Together they revived mass nationalist agitation after the quiet period (1908-1915). The founding month distinction (Tilak: April; Besant: September) and the city distinction (Tilak: Poona; Besant: Adyar/Madras) are the specific tested details — swapping Tilak with Besant or their cities is the standard trap.
questions.source: The two Home Rule Leagues (Tilak April/Poona vs. Besant September/Adyar) is one of the most frequently tested 'which came first / which HQ' questions in the 1916 period of SSC CGL Modern History

questions.text: The song 'Vande Mataram' became the battle cry of the Swadeshi Movement. Which of the following is correct about this song?
options: It was composed by Rabindranath Tagore in 1905 specifically for the anti-Partition movement
It was composed by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee and appeared in his novel Anandamath (1882); it was adopted as the movement's anthem during the Swadeshi movement (1905)
It was composed by Tilak in Marathi and translated to Bengali for the Swadeshi movement
It was originally an Urdu poem that Tagore translated to Bengali for the Rakhi Bandhan ceremony
questions.explanation: Vande Mataram was composed by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee and first appeared in his Bengali novel Anandamath (1882) — a fictional account of a Hindu monastic order fighting Mughal rule. The song is addressed to the motherland, personified as a goddess. During the Swadeshi movement (1905), it was adopted as the anthem for the anti-Partition agitation and became so powerful that the British eventually banned its public singing. It was NOT composed in 1905 — it was an existing song from 1882 that the movement adopted. Rabindranath Tagore set it to music for public performance, but the song itself is Bankim's. 'Amar Sonar Bangla' (which became Bangladesh's national anthem) was Tagore's composition specifically for the movement.
questions.source: Vande Mataram (Bankim Chandra Chatterjee; Anandamath, 1882) vs. Tagore compositions is a frequently tested distinction; many students incorrectly attribute Vande Mataram to Tagore
